result:
:::writing
By the time the streetlights flickered on, the city had already decided what kind of night it wanted to be.

It chose a soft one—edges blurred by humidity, conversations leaking out of open windows, a low hum of traffic that sounded less like movement and more like a promise that something, somewhere, was still happening. The kind of night where nothing important was supposed to occur, which is exactly why something always did.

Mara walked without a destination, which wasn’t unusual for her, but tonight felt different in a way she couldn’t name. Her shoes made a quiet rhythm against the pavement, steady and unhurried, like she was keeping time for a song she hadn’t heard yet.

She had left her phone at home on purpose.

At first, it had felt like forgetting a limb. Phantom vibrations haunted her pockets. She kept reaching for a screen that wasn’t there, like her mind was trying to check in with something that might tell her who she was supposed to be in that moment. But after a while, something loosened. The world felt less filtered, less mediated. The silence wasn’t empty—it was crowded with small details she usually missed.

A man arguing gently with a parking meter. A stray cat watching everything like it was taking notes. The faint smell of someone cooking garlic three floors up. Life, happening without asking her to document it.

She turned onto a quieter street, where the buildings leaned closer together, as if sharing secrets. Halfway down the block, she noticed the light.

It wasn’t dramatic. No flicker, no strange color. Just a warm, steady glow spilling from a storefront that, as far as she knew, had been empty for months. The windows were clean, which was new, and behind them she could see shelves—wooden, uneven, filled with objects she couldn’t quite make out from the sidewalk.

Curiosity, as always, got the better of her.

The door opened with a small bell that sounded more like a question than an announcement.

Inside, the air was cooler, and the noise of the city softened, like someone had turned the volume down just for this room. The place smelled faintly of paper and something herbal—rosemary, maybe, or thyme.

“Hello?” Mara called.

No answer.

She stepped further in, her eyes adjusting. The shelves were lined with things that didn’t seem to belong together: old cameras, stacks of handwritten letters tied with twine, small glass jars filled with indistinct contents, watches that had all stopped at different times.

There were labels, but they weren’t helpful in any practical sense.

“Tuesday, but the good kind.”

“Something you almost remembered.”

“The last five minutes before it mattered.”

Mara smiled despite herself. “Okay,” she murmured. “That’s new.”

“Not really.”

The voice came from behind her, calm and unhurried.

She turned. The person standing there looked… ordinary. Not in a disappointing way, just in a way that resisted description. If she tried to remember them later, she suspected she wouldn’t be able to say much beyond “they were there.”

“Sorry,” Mara said. “I didn’t see anyone. I thought this place was empty.”

“It usually is,” the person replied, moving behind a counter that she hadn’t noticed before. “Until it isn’t.”

“That’s not how stores work.”

“It is for this one.”

Mara glanced back at the shelves. “What is this place?”

The person considered the question like it deserved a thoughtful answer. “It’s where things end up when they’re not finished being what they are.”

“That doesn’t mean anything.”

“It doesn’t mean nothing, either.”

She let out a quiet laugh. “Do you always talk like that?”

“Only when I’m being honest.”

Mara picked up a small object from the nearest shelf—a pocket watch, its surface worn smooth. The label read: “Three minutes you didn’t use.”

“What does this do?” she asked.

“It holds time you lost without noticing.”

“That sounds like all time.”

“Not quite. Some of it you give away on purpose. Some of it is taken from you. This”—the person gestured toward the watch—“is the kind that slips through because you thought it didn’t matter.”

Mara turned the watch over in her hand. It was heavier than she expected.

“What happens if I open it?”

“You get those three minutes back.”

She raised an eyebrow. “And they’re… what? Random?”

“They’re yours. They just weren’t claimed.”

Mara hesitated. “And if I don’t open it?”

“Then they stay here. Someone else might use them. Or not.”

“That seems unfair.”

“Only if you think time belongs to you in the first place.”

She set the watch down carefully. “This is weird.”

“Yes.”

“And you’re okay with that?”

The person smiled faintly. “It’s consistent.”

Mara moved deeper into the shop, drawn to a shelf near the back. A glass jar caught her attention. Inside was something that looked like light, but not quite—more like the memory of light.

The label read: “The moment you almost said it.”

Her chest tightened, just a little.

“What’s in this one?” she asked, even though she had a feeling she already knew.

The person’s voice was softer now. “A conversation you stopped before it could change things.”

Mara didn’t touch the jar. “Can I… see it?”

“You can experience it. It’s not something you watch from the outside.”

“And then what?”

“Then you’ll know what would have happened if you hadn’t held back.”

“That sounds dangerous.”

“It can be.”

“Do people regret it?”

“Sometimes. But they also regret not knowing.”

Mara stared at the jar. The light inside shifted, almost imperceptibly, like it was reacting to her attention.

She thought about all the things she had left unsaid. The arguments she had softened into silence. The confessions she had swallowed because they felt too heavy, too risky, too final.

Three minutes you didn’t use.

The moment you almost said it.

“How much does it cost?” she asked.

The person behind the counter tilted their head. “You don’t pay with money.”

“Of course not.”

“You pay with certainty.”

Mara frowned. “What does that mean?”

“It means once you know, you can’t go back to not knowing. You lose the version of yourself that still had a question instead of an answer.”

“That sounds like a bad deal.”

“Only if you prefer wondering over understanding.”

She let that sit.

Outside, a car passed, its headlights briefly cutting through the window and casting moving shadows across the walls. For a moment, everything in the shop seemed to shift—objects catching the light differently, labels becoming almost readable in a more literal way before slipping back into ambiguity.

Mara took a slow breath.

“Do people ever leave without taking anything?” she asked.

“All the time.”

“And they’re fine?”

“They’re exactly as they were before.”

She nodded. “That’s what I thought.”

Her hand hovered near the jar, close enough to feel a faint warmth radiating from the glass.

“Okay,” she said quietly. “But what if I don’t like what I see?”

The person met her gaze. “Then you’ll have learned something true.”

Mara exhaled, long and steady.

“Yeah,” she said. “That’s the problem.”

For a moment, neither of them moved.

Then, slowly, she pulled her hand back.

“Not tonight,” she said.

The person nodded, as if this outcome had always been one of the likely ones. “That’s fine.”

Mara glanced around the shop one more time, trying to fix it in her memory—the strange labels, the impossible objects, the feeling that she had stepped slightly outside the usual rules of things.

“Will this place be here tomorrow?” she asked.

“Maybe.”

“That’s not helpful.”

“It’s accurate.”

She smiled, just a little. “Right.”

When she stepped back outside, the city felt louder than before, but also clearer. The hum of it, the movement, the small, unnoticed details—they were all still there, waiting.

She reached instinctively for her phone, then remembered it wasn’t with her.

For once, that didn’t bother her.

Mara started walking again, the rhythm of her steps picking up slightly, like she had found the song after all.

Behind her, the storefront light dimmed, then disappeared entirely, leaving nothing but an empty window and the faint impression that something had almost happened.

Which, in its own way, was something.
:::writing

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
